entry.title: Thinking About the Essence: Universe, Humanity, and the Self - Discovering Life's Meaning and Inner Peace
entry.content:
In my daily reflections, I often ponder profound questions about the universe, essence, and self. I frequently look up at the stars, contemplating the nature of the universe and our place in this vast space. These seemingly distant questions are closely related to our everyday lives.

The Essence and Mysteries of the Universe: Our Place in the Vastness

Every time I gaze at the stars, I am struck by the vastness and mystery of the universe. Although science tells us the universe began with the Big Bang, its origins and future remain full of enigmas. What we know may be just the tip of the iceberg; exploring the universe is an endless journey.

The Nature of Humanity and the Evolution of Civilization: Who Are We?

The development of human civilization evokes complex feelings in me. On one hand, we have created rich cultures and advanced technologies, transforming the world. On the other hand, we face challenges like environmental pollution and social issues. This makes me question what human nature is. Is our civilization truly progressing? These questions drive me to delve into the past, present, and future of humanity.

Exploring Self-Discovery: What Kind of Being Am I?

Now, let’s talk about the self. I often ask myself, who am I? Why do I think and act the way I do? Self-discovery is a complex and evolving process. I realize that my understanding of myself changes over time. Every attempt to understand myself feels like chasing a shadow—confusing yet fascinating.

The Purpose of Life and the Pursuit of Fulfillment: What Constitutes True Inner Satisfaction?

When it comes to the meaning of life, I often reflect. Some people believe that the meaning of life lies in pursuing happiness, but what is happiness? In this challenging world, I have come to understand that the meaning of life may lie in seeking inner satisfaction and peace. This satisfaction does not come from material wealth but from inner harmony and self-realization.

Finding Inner Peace: Achieving Calm in a Busy Life

Finally, I consider inner peace. In today’s fast-paced world, finding inner calm is not easy. However, through simple habits, such as taking time each day to reflect or engage in activities I enjoy, I find brief moments of tranquility. This peace is not an escape from reality but learning to coexist harmoniously with oneself.

Related Articles:

  • Exploring the Essence of the Universe
  • Self-Discovery and the Meaning of Life

References:

  • Universe - Wikipedia
  • Humanity - Wikipedia
  • Self - Wikipedia
  • Purpose of Life - Wikipedia
  • Inner Peace - Wikipedia
entry.summary: Reflecting on the essence of the universe, human nature, and self-discovery, thinking about the meaning of life and how to find inner peace in a complex world.

entry.title: The Power of Simplicity: Understanding Complexity Through Simplicity
entry.content:
Many years ago, I might have been someone who delved into various fields of knowledge, and perhaps I still am to this day, but now I’m captivated by a very simple principle: “Keep it simple.”

This principle, originally known as “K.I.S.S” - Keep it simple, stupid.

Albert Einstein once said:

“Everything should be made as simple as possible, but no simpler.”

“It can scarcely be denied that the supreme goal of all theory is to make the irreducible basic elements as simple and as few as possible without having to surrender the adequate representation of a single datum of experience.”

For many things, we often think they are too complex, too complex to understand. In fact, the world can be broken down into simplicity. Zhang Chaoyang once shared this insight.

I recall Fu Peirong discussing Western philosophy in class, mentioning one of Heraclitus’ thoughts: “All things come from fire.”

This perspective may seem erroneous now, but at that time, I found it impressive. He was seeking the most fundamental construction of the world and drew a conclusion.

Perhaps this is one of the origins of materialism, and it’s quite significant.

When discussing logic, we also need to clarify the concept first, as the concept is the most basic unit of semantic understanding.

Back during my student days, when I first encountered chemical molecules and atoms, one day our chemistry teacher told us that both diamond and charcoal are composed of carbon elements. However, diamond is C60, and charcoal is C4, just differing in structural bonds.

In the workplace, we often break down tasks in planning, aiming to simplify tasks to the utmost, to the point where they are crystal clear.

The periodic table of elements is a marvelous thing, and it remains so to this day. Bill Gates has an element wall in his office, reportedly collecting physical samples of all elements except radioactive ones.

I’ve also attempted to understand the simplicity of this world, trying to peel away the layers of its various exteriors.

The world originally might have consisted of individual entities composed of these elements, and then due to various connections, the world gained its rich diversity, forming its shape.

More than 90% of things in this world can be broken down to a simple level of comprehension. If they cannot, it only suggests that we are still unable to understand, unable to delve into the truth and essence, requiring continual exploration.

The world of computers has only 0 and 1. Is it possible that our world is the same?

Related Articles:

  • Understanding Complexity Through Simplicity
  • Philosophical Insights on Simplicity

References:

  • Albert Einstein - Wikipedia
  • Heraclitus - Wikipedia
entry.summary: Explore how the principle of 'Keep it simple' reveals the underlying simplicity behind complex concepts and theories.

entry.title: The Deepest Wisdom in Jet Li's Journey: Yin Yang, Science & Pure Dreams
entry.content:
Lately, intermittently, I’ve come across a classic interview by Wang Lifen about Jet Li. When I watched the complete video again, I found myself reflecting on a multitude of topics—thinking, the One Foundation, the Internet, humanity, faith, and so much more. This led to a new understanding and sentiment.

Jet Li’s Early Understanding of Yin Yang

Jet Li understood the concept of Yin Yang when he was only 11 years old, grasping the essence of Tai Chi. It wasn’t until he was around 30 that he began to glimpse the truth: the world isn’t just black and white.

The Relationship Between Science and Truth

Speaking of science, Jet Li shared:

"The mysteries of the universe that humanity has mastered over five thousand years are truly limited. The scientific knowledge we possess is quite confined; it's called science precisely because it isn't scientific."

I never expected to hear this from the Emperor of Kung Fu, but it’s truly remarkable that someone who didn’t graduate from elementary school, who has remained humble about his lack of education, could arrive at such a profound realization.

Regarding science, many people nowadays lack an understanding, thinking that science equates to truth. In fact, it’s not. The correctness of science is temporary, and it’s through constant negation and revision that we arrive at truth. There might not even be any absolute truth in this world. The only truth might be the continuous process of seeking the truth.

Exploring Zen Buddhism and the Path of Dao

Due to martial arts and the film “Shaolin Temple,” Jet Li gained insight into the world, comprehending movies and the universe. As a teenager, he developed an entirely new perception of the world, which he continually pondered. He questioned why people always said, “Martial artists are simple-minded with well-developed limbs.” He spent half his life seeking evidence and disproving this notion, dedicating himself to learning to cultivate as much wisdom as possible, which he indeed achieved.

This realization motivated Jet Li to leave the system, even if it meant breaking his leg. His determination led to a serendipitous leg injury, and he found himself ecstatic about it, sensing that fate was on his side. Perhaps it was youthful ignorance or youthful aspiration that led him to such an outlook. He ventured out, giving birth to numerous exceptional films, but films were merely a fraction of his excellence.

He is someone who contemplates deeply: he reflects on whether something is problematic when everyone does it. He ponders how to be more efficient. These two ways of thinking became deeply ingrained in him at a young age, perhaps influenced by his early encounter with the concept of yin and yang. It’s remarkable; although exposed to this concept early, not everyone can grasp its essence.

Fortunately, after his introduction to Zen Buddhism, the constant yearning led him to the edge of Dao, a stroke of immense luck.

The Pursuit of Pure Dreams

He continuously reads books, biographies of famous people, the life stories of Marie Curie, Stalin, Hitler, Edison, and selections from Deng Xiaoping, among others. He reads classics, including the Bible and the Quran.

When he was young, Jet Li had a dream—to water flowers. However, when he shared this dream with those around him, they labeled him as lacking ambition, direction, and courage, accusing him of being a coward who didn’t aspire for greatness.

The Little Prince once had such a pure dream, unable to comprehend why so many people were so complicated, so impure.

And now, what I’m tirelessly pursuing is nothing more than a pure dream, akin to watering flowers—perhaps one or several dreams.

Related Articles:

  • Exploring the Concept of Yin Yang
  • Zen Buddhism: Understanding the Way

References:

  • Jet Li - Wikipedia
  • Yin Yang - Wikipedia
entry.summary: Explore the profound wisdom in Jet Li's journey through Yin Yang, Zen Buddhism, and his pursuit of pure dreams and science.

entry.title: Assuming the World Is Comprehensible, Where Lies Its Comprehensibility?
entry.content:
Comprehension

The most incomprehensible thing about the world is that it is comprehensible.
— Albert Einstein

Albert Einstein’s insight into 「comprehension」 and its applicability to the world prompts us to ask: What exactly did he mean?

Was his perspective rooted in quantum mechanics, probabilistic randomness, or something else? Although the exact origins of this quote are debated, its core question remains: Where does the 「domain」 of “comprehension” lie?

Einstein famously said, “God does not play dice.”

However, the behavior of microscopic particles suggests that randomness and probability are intrinsic to our world. This randomness extends to the macroscopic world, where countless decisions and actions are influenced by complex factors and conditions.

Socrates

All men are mortal;
Socrates is a man;
Therefore, Socrates is mortal.

Consider this: What if probability were integrated into inductive and deductive logic?

99% of all men are mortal;
Socrates has an 80% chance of being a man;
What is the likelihood of Socrates being mortal?

Randomness vs. Uncertainty

The distinction between randomness and uncertainty is crucial:

Randomness means that while the possible outcomes of an event are known, the specific outcome remains unpredictable.

Previously, I thought of this as process thinking—believing that understanding the entire process or having a divine perspective would make things appear natural.

However, reality often defies this view. For example, why do diverse ideologies and groups exist? Even the existence of Earth might seem random.

Our efforts to understand the future involve transforming uncertainties into randomness. Most aspects of the world are random rather than uncertain.

God might enjoy playing dice, but knowing that the dice only have numbers 1 through 6 is a different matter from not knowing if the dice could have numbers beyond 6. The latter represents uncertainty, which can be unsettling.

The Endpoint of Probability Is 1

Probability has three key properties:

  1. Probability ranges between 0 and 1; it cannot be negative.
  2. The sum of the probabilities of all basic events in the sample space is 1.
  3. The probability of an event not occurring is 1 minus the probability of it occurring.

The second property is particularly intriguing: the sum of probabilities equals 1, or 100%. This “1” signifies two things:

  1. The sample must be complete and without omissions, a challenging task in reality.
  2. Probability represents a necessary event within a system, but it is contingent upon specific details.

To maximize the likelihood of an event, one must thoroughly understand it.
entry.summary: Exploring the nature of comprehension in a probabilistic world and the distinction between randomness and uncertainty.

entry.title: Why Do Humans Ask "Why"?
entry.content:
Seeing mountains as mountains, and water as water;

Seeing mountains as not mountains, and water as not water;

Seeing mountains still as mountains, and water still as water.

At first, humans might not have asked “why.” But when did humans consciously start asking “why”? This is the origin.

Why can the 「2W1H」 framework help us think through certain problems, and why does this framework clarify some matters?

What is What, what is Why, and what is How?

Why are there three, and why are these three?

We are thinking, but we are rarely aware that we are thinking.

Where is the source of thought? In the brain.

Where is the source of the brain? In consciousness.

One of philosophy’s focuses might be on the origin.

If humans ask why, what might result? They might obtain an effect.

If humans don’t ask why, what might happen? They might just follow a predetermined path.

We continuously strive to explore the origin of this world, constantly asking why in order to understand the world clearly. We are all exploring the rationality of existence. There is always something implicit behind this: 「causal logic.」

It’s difficult for us to explain how the brain expressed and understood the world before consciousness, how children understand the world, how they think, or if they even think at all. Is our thinking as adults a kind of imprisoned thinking?

This world seems to have a “cause” for everything. The moment humans ask a question, there might be an unalterable belief within consciousness: belief in “causality.”

I still vividly remember a scene from “Divine Providence” where Han Xin tries to understand and believe in the existence of aliens. Later, in “New Song,” I was amazed by Wang Anshi’s awareness that even if someone knew the future, it wouldn’t be enough to change anything.

A possible law of this world is that 「everything has a cause and effect.」

But, is that true?

Does an absolute belief in causality prevent you from truly understanding the root of the world? Just as what we call absolute might just be a special case of a probabilistic event, this world might be primarily composed of dark matter, with matter being only a part of it.

When what we firmly believe to be the essence is merely a facade, will you still believe in this world?
entry.summary:
Seeing mountains as mountains, and water as water;

Seeing mountains as not mountains, and water as not water;

entry.title: Many Times, What We Say is Just a Cliché
entry.content:
Seek Truth from Facts

A phrase can easily become a cliché if it’s repeated so often that its meaning is lost. This was illustrated during a high school debate I participated in. Both sides frequently mentioned the term worldview. At one point, an opponent asked, “What exactly is a worldview?” We were caught off guard and unable to provide a clear explanation. The entire class, including our teacher, applauded the question, highlighting our lack of depth on the topic.

This experience made me realize that many concepts we think we understand and discuss are often just clichés—repeated without genuine insight.

What Does “Seek Truth from Facts” Mean?

The Book of Han states: “Study diligently, love antiquity, and seek truth from facts.”

Mao Zedong further clarified: “Facts” refer to all objectively existing things. “Truth” refers to the internal connections or regularities of these things. “Seek” means to study and understand them.

In problem-solving, we often rely on subjective perceptions, experiences, and sometimes unverified sources. Differentiating between facts and opinions is crucial. True critical thinking is grounded in objective facts rather than subjective viewpoints.

Understanding the world requires us to clarify what we truly know and believe. By focusing on objective facts, many problems can be resolved more effectively.
entry.summary: Explore the concept of clichés and the importance of seeking truth from facts in understanding worldviews and solving problems.

entry.title: Useless and Uselessness
entry.content:
“Dao”

This weekend, I revisited the TV series The Dao and came across a profound discussion about the concept of “Dao”:

———

Ding Yuanying said: “There are three levels to understanding society: technology, systems, and culture. Whether for an individual or a nation, any fate ultimately stems from cultural attributes. Dominant cultures create strong individuals, while weak cultures produce weak ones. This is a rule, and it can be understood as **the Dao, which is not subject to human will.**”

“Dominant cultures follow the laws of things, while weak cultures rely on the moral expectations of the strong, seeking salvation. Dominant cultures are like ‘secret manuals’ in martial arts, while weak cultures, being easier to learn, understand, and use, become popular.”

Rui Xiaodan asked: “I can understand this from the literal sense, but how does it apply?”

Ding Yuanying replied: “**Useless and Uselessness.**”

———

The phrase “Useless and Uselessness” precisely addresses the answer to my recent intellectual dilemma.

The term “Dao” represents the principles I have long sought, things that are not subject to personal will, which could be laws, ways of thinking, or various forms.

Through prolonged exploration, I have come to believe in the foundational role of Formal Logic and the worldview of Marxist Philosophy.

Even though I have established logic and Marxist philosophy as foundational driving forces, I still found myself confused in practical application. Perhaps this is because the process of applying these theories in practice often brings new insights.

I have questioned how logic and philosophy can be applied in real life. Rui Xiaodan’s question, “What’s the use?” highlights this concern.

Useless might seem to have no direct application, but Uselessness aligns with a belief I have long held, although I had not fully committed to it before.

The boundary of Uselessness lies in the fact that logic and philosophy can indeed be applied to various aspects of daily life and work.

I feel fortunate to have gained some insight in my 30s. This insight has provided a clear answer from the “Dao”. The answer has been within me all along, only needing a reason to believe and persist.

An interesting question is: Humans often seek meaning in their actions, but is this meaning truly important?

I’m still not sure, but for many things, it’s crucial for me to understand “why”.

The concept of “Useless and Uselessness” embodies the principle of simplicity. What seems simple is actually challenging to achieve, while complex things can be simplified, often due to our misunderstanding or overcomplication of the unknown.

Laozi said: “Wu wei but nothing is not done.”

Following the “Dao”, seeking truth from facts, and observing the cause and effect in our actions.
entry.summary:
“Dao”

This weekend, I revisited the TV series The Dao and came across a profound discussion about the concept of “Dao”:</

entry.title: The Dao in the Balance of Yin and Yang
entry.content:
The Dao in the Balance of Yin and Yang

The ancient saying goes: “The Dao is the balance of Yin and Yang.”

The quest for perfection and persistent self-reflection can validate our existence and lead to profound self-awareness.

In developing my personal knowledge system using Flomo, I have experienced several stages:

  1. Classic Notes: Minimal reflection, random keyword tags, no classification.
  2. Increased Reflection: Less note-taking, categorized tags [P.A.R.A], structured expression.
  3. Contrasting Thinking: Examining facts vs. opinions, rational vs. emotional, objective vs. subjective, explicit vs. implicit, self vs. others, individual vs. society, ordered vs. disordered, positive vs. negative, etc.
  4. Form and Content: Reflecting on form vs. content, logical thinking, tracing philosophy, and conceptual thinking.
  5. Exploration and Simplification: Contemplating the present vs. the beyond, exploring simplicity, pursuing an ideal realm.
  6. Abstract and Deductive: Thinking in abstract vs. deductive terms.
  7. …

Currently, my reflections have yet to reach the ideal pinnacle of Dao or the metaphorical boundary of understanding. Reading the profound insights of ancient sages fills me with immense joy and a deep sense of gratitude.

In Season 2 of KQ Walk the World, there is a hypothesis: What if deserts disappeared from Earth?

Transforming deserts into forests might seem like a grand achievement, but deserts play a crucial role in the global climate cycle. Removing them could potentially create new ecological problems for the Earth.

Are justice and evil absolute? Is right and wrong truly important? Is there a gray area between black and white? Are rational numbers finite? Do irrational numbers have an end? Why is energy conserved? Why does entropy continue to increase?

The world of computers consists of only 0 and 1, while the real world is a balance of Yin and Yang, constantly rotating and alternating.

Perhaps the boundary between reality and virtuality is merely a matter of perception. What defines the point where quantity transforms into quality?
entry.summary: Explore the concept of Dao through the balance of Yin and Yang, reflecting on knowledge systems and the nature of reality.

entry.title: What You Focus on Echoes Back
entry.content:
Rebirth

A classic philosophical paradox is Theseus’ Ship: If the wooden planks of Theseus’ ship are gradually replaced until none of the original planks remain, is it still the same ship?

Some say yes, others say no. If our cells regenerate daily, and eventually, all of our cells are replaced, are we still the same person? And if our thoughts and perceptions evolve every day, are we still the same self?

Perhaps the answer doesn’t matter. What truly matters is that life continues, and new beginnings emerge.

Material conditions shape consciousness, but consciousness also impacts material reality. A new understanding can lead to a form of rebirth.

In English, the prefix “re” signifies repetition or renewal, as seen in words like reborn, rework, rebuild, review, restart, and recall.

In the digital age, technological advancements continuously reinvent industries. From web1.0 to web2.0, and now the emerging web3.0, each phase has marked a rebirth of various sectors.

For individuals, many of our perceptions are shaped by our upbringing and environment. However, these perceptions aren’t always accurate. Human behavior is driven by habits, which in turn are shaped by consciousness. Therefore, should we not re-examine our past knowledge, concepts, and beliefs?

This might be a lengthy journey, but certain key ideas are worth reassessing. Over the past few years, I have sought the root of specific issues, recently finding answers in two textbooks: Formal Logic and Marxist Philosophy. Perhaps my college exposure to these subjects has led me to this path of introspection.

In essence, it’s a re-evaluation of the Three Views: worldview, values, and outlook on life.

Marxist philosophy posits that the relationship between humans and the world centers around two main activities: understanding the world and transforming it. All human activities are focused on these two goals. This also explains why “carving oneself and benefiting others” is a fundamental drive and value. First, we must understand ourselves and the world, and then we transform it.

Everyone has the opportunity for rebirth, but it requires a re-examination of oneself and the world—a baptism of the Three Views.

In my value system, only through a deep understanding of oneself and the world can we find a peaceful path forward.
entry.summary: Explore the philosophical concept of rebirth and how our focus and perceptions shape our understanding of the world and ourselves.

entry.title: The Process of Thinking Involves the Use of Concepts, Judgments, and Reasoning
entry.content:
The Process of Thinking Involves the Use of Concepts, Judgments, and Reasoning

We often discuss “logic,” but what exactly does it mean?

The term “logic” originates from the Greek word λογος (logos), which means thought, reason, principle, word, etc. In everyday language, “logic” can be understood in three distinct ways:

1. The Regularity of Interconnection: It refers to the regularity in the interconnection and development of objective things and the regularity of human thinking that reflects this. For example, “the logic of the Chinese revolution” or “the logic of things.” Here, “logic” pertains to the regularity of objective phenomena. Similarly, “drawing a logical conclusion” or describing an article as having strong logic refers to the regularity in thinking.

2. A Theory, Viewpoint, or Method: It represents a specific theory, viewpoint, or method of reasoning. For instance, when invaders label their aggression as “friendship,” we criticize it as “pure robber logic.” In this context, “logic” refers to the distorted reasoning or method of understanding.

3. The Science of Correct Thinking: It denotes the study of the forms and laws of correct thinking, including formal logic and dialectical logic, though commonly it refers to formal logic. For example, “learning grammar and logic” or “high school students studying logic.” Here, “logic” signifies the science of formal logic.

What is Formal Logic?

Formal logic studies the forms and laws of thinking and is primarily a science of thinking. Engels described logic as the doctrine of the laws governing the thinking process.

Understanding Thinking

Mao Zedong, in his “On Practice,” described the cognitive process: People initially perceive only the phenomena of things, their one-sidedness, and external connections. This represents the perceptual stage of cognition, where profound concepts or logical conclusions are not yet formed. As social practice continues, repeated experiences lead to a transformation in cognition, resulting in concept formation.

Recently, I found this explanation of the origin of “concepts” particularly insightful.

The thinking process is an abstract and indirect reflection of the objective world, characterized by abstraction and indirectness. This reflection is mediated through language.

The process of thinking involves concepts, judgments, and reasoning—essential forms of thinking used to reflect objective reality, which logic refers to as forms of thought.

———

The content above is adapted from the book Formal Logic, compiled by the Department of Philosophy at East China Normal University. The book is now in its fifth edition, having significantly improved from its first edition drafted in 1963.

My interest in this book stems from a desire to understand the world accurately. Formal logic addresses the formal laws of thinking, regardless of content. This aligns with my exploration of whether a fixed paradigm exists for reflecting the objective world subjectively. To me, logic encompasses three core elements: concepts, judgments, and reasoning.

While science may not always be perfect, it currently represents the worldview I adhere to. I believe that the Chinese education system could benefit from a broader emphasis on logic. In the future, I might even advocate for the importance of logic in education.

Ultimately, understanding the world involves grasping the dialectical relationship between oneself and the world—a reciprocal, spiraling journey from the mundane to the profound, interacting between consciousness and matter.
entry.summary: Explore the various meanings of logic, its role in formal and dialectical thinking, and the importance of understanding concepts, judgments, and reasoning.

entry.title: Habit is a Behavior, and Behavior is Easier to Change
entry.content:
Habit is a Behavior, and Behavior is Easier to Change

A few days ago, I came across a thought-provoking psychology video on Douyin from “This Thought Official” titled “What Should You Never Say to Your Child?” The insights shared were quite impactful.

Guest Huang Shiming highlighted the importance of avoiding negative language at the identity level. For instance, if a client says, “Hey, teacher, I’m an alcoholic,” seeking psychological counseling, my response would be different. Instead of agreeing with the label, I would say, “Are you saying that you have a habit of drinking?”

Habit is a behavior, and behavior is easier to change.

Similarly, if someone says, “I’m a depressed person,” my response would be, “Are you saying you smile a little less than others?”

If my child expresses doubt with statements like, “Dad, I’m sure I’ll fail. I identify as someone who can’t succeed,” I would counter with, “Oh, are you saying that this time, you might fail?”

This approach helps to relax the tension and opens up a space for improvement and new possibilities. Even if the exam doesn’t go well, the focus shifts to a supportive alliance, aiming for gradual improvement. This perspective helps build trust and support.

The Impact of Labeling

Often, definitive labels such as “I’m a loser” or “He’s a bad person” can impose a heavy, invisible burden. These labels can be suffocating and, if not addressed, may create a vicious cycle of self-fulfilling prophecies.

Life is not confined by rigid boundaries, and we shouldn’t be limited by simplistic definitions. The world is complex and nuanced, with more shades than just black and white. Embracing this complexity and maintaining a positive mindset can lead to significant changes over time.

Video Source
entry.summary: Explore how labeling and defining oneself or others can limit potential and how shifting to view habits as behaviors can facilitate change.

entry.title: I Think, Therefore I Am
entry.content:
Descartes once said, “I think, therefore I am.”

Many years ago, I asked a question on Zhihu about how to understand Hegel’s phrase “What is real is rational.” For years, it seems I’ve been chasing the question: If my existence is rational, then why do I exist, and what makes my existence different?

Life, at times, seems to be a series of hypotheses—some societal, some personal. What follows is an endless pursuit and exploration surrounding these assumptions.

Lately, I’ve been reading two books: The Essence and The Power of Thinking. My interest in the concept of “essence” sparked this journey. Most of us seek to understand the essence of things, but it’s often elusive. So why can some people perceive the essence, while others cannot?

Most people recognize the importance of thinking—independent thinking, critical thinking, deep thinking, and reverse thinking. Yet, despite various thought processes being distilled over time, humanity has never fully explained the act of thinking itself, such as what consciousness is.

The Power of Thinking offers this definition of thinking: Thinking refers to the cognitive process of analyzing and judging one or more external objects to arrive at specific conclusions about them.

One question I’ve pondered for a long time is, “How can we understand the world?”

It’s a process that reflects the world onto the individual, moving from the objective world to the subjective, and from this shore to the other shore. It’s challenging but worth a lifetime of thought.

What you see in the world may not be true. It requires continuous verification to determine what is real. Some find this pursuit dull, but I find it deeply meaningful.

Biologically, thinking is essentially the result of nerve impulses between neurons, leading to reflexes and other behaviors.

We think every day, passively as information floods in, actively when faced with new problems, and sometimes unconsciously, even when our minds seem to wander.

But how should we think? How can we make our thinking more efficient? How can we turn our thoughts into actions?

While thinking may have no end, it can have dimensions—depth, breadth, boundaries, and stages. Thinking can even become reflexive, where we think about thinking itself—meta-thinking.

Sometimes understanding the rules of the world is simple, just a short journey from the object itself to the brain’s specific response. Yet this brief path is fraught with challenges, and one misstep could lead you astray, away from your intended destination.

In my view, human civilization persists because it is the crystallization of continuous thought and exploration by those who came before us. Sometimes I wonder, when you fully understand the thought processes of past greats, is that person who thinks just like them still you?

Schopenhauer said, “The man who thinks stands as the sovereign of his own spiritual kingdom.” May every thoughtful person reign over their own world and become the ruler of their own life.

[1] Ai Fei. The Essence Link

[2] Lüska. The Power of Thinking Link
entry.summary:
Descartes once said, “I think, therefore I am.”

Many years ago, I asked a question on Zhihu about how to understand Hegel’s phrase

entry.title: Supporting Role or Leading Role? Understanding Life's Roles
entry.content:
Supporting Role or Leading Role? Understanding Life’s Roles

The Journey of Playing Different Roles

Life is a journey of playing various roles, often shifting from being a main character to a supporting role or even a mere passerby. Many people realize, sometimes suddenly, that they may not be the protagonist they once thought they were.

As a child, I admired the main characters in movies, but as I grew older, I began to appreciate the growth and significance of supporting roles. This shift in perspective helped me understand the value of every role, regardless of its prominence.

Seizing the Moment

Imagine life as a play where you only have 10 seconds to make an impact. How would you seize that moment? We play numerous roles throughout our lives—child, student, employee, lover, parent, and many more. But do we truly understand the responsibilities and meanings behind these roles?

Reflecting on Personal Roles

My own reflection on roles deepened when I was first promoted to director. Initially, I was thrilled by the promotion and the raise, but soon realized that managing both up and down was far more challenging than anticipated. My initial struggles with prioritization and personal management led to criticism and a disordered team dynamic.

It wasn’t until I fully understood what it meant to be a “director” that I began to manage effectively. By setting clear boundaries and focusing on essential tasks, I was able to improve the situation. Adapting quickly to new roles is crucial to avoid exhaustion and inefficiency for both yourself and your colleagues.

The Essence of Different Roles

Understanding what it means to be an employee, manager, child, parent, student, teacher, friend, or lover involves more than just fulfilling duties. It requires proactive thinking, responsibility, and effective communication.

A true manager, for example, goes beyond merely giving orders. They must guide, support, and sometimes outshine even the specialists in their team. Similarly, in personal relationships, understanding the boundaries and expectations of each role is essential.

The Role of Attentiveness

A recent viral video highlighted this idea vividly. In a war game, one team’s commander fled, and a Chinese player stepped up to lead with bilingual instructions, turning the tide of the game. This player, initially just a soldier, embraced the role of commander when needed, demonstrating the importance of attentiveness and responsibility.

Embracing Assigned Roles

Many roles in life are assigned to us, but it’s our responsibility to excel in them. If you were given a 10-second role, would you be ready to make the most of it?

As Wong Jing noted, Stephen Chow perfected seven different expressions in a 10-second scene in A Chinese Odyssey. This effort underscores the significance of every role, no matter how brief or seemingly insignificant.

By understanding and embracing the roles assigned to us, we can navigate life’s journey with greater clarity and effectiveness.
entry.summary: Explore the significance of various life roles, from being a supporting character to a leading role, and how to understand and embrace these roles effectively.

entry.title: I Am What I Am: Understanding Concepts and Personal Breakthroughs
entry.content:
I Am What I Am: Understanding Concepts and Personal Breakthroughs

The Role of Formal Logic

Formal Logic defines a concept as a form of thought reflecting the unique attributes or essential properties of an object. This understanding is crucial for personal breakthroughs and effective communication.

The Quest for Personal Breakthroughs

In the pursuit of personal breakthroughs, continuous learning and organizing knowledge are essential. However, daily problems and doubts often persist. Despite efforts in cognitive awakening and building knowledge systems, achieving clarity can be challenging.

Communication and Conceptual Understanding

In professional settings, discrepancies often arise between what is communicated and the final outcome. Reflecting on these issues often reveals misunderstandings in conceptual comprehension. Einstein’s observation, “The most incomprehensible thing about the world is that it is comprehensible,” underscores the importance of understanding concepts deeply.

The Paradigm of Understanding Through Concepts

To grasp the world effectively, one must understand the “concepts” at play. Concepts are distinguished by their “characteristics,” which clarify many problems. The internet frequently notes that if a product cannot be explained in one sentence, it lacks clarity—this principle applies to both business positioning and logical definition.

Improving Expression and Logic

Many struggle with expressing themselves clearly due to unclear concepts and logic. To address this, I use a method of note-taking with Flomo, recording “keywords.” This approach helps with indexing and practices two essential logical skills: deduction and abstraction.

  • Deduction involves expanding on a concept, defining it, and providing examples.
  • Abstraction simplifies complex matters by extracting commonalities and being concise.

Conceptual Thinking

At the NoteMan Annual Conference, Teacher Ke Zhou highlighted “conceptual thinking,” a method of identifying essential characteristics of seemingly unrelated things. Huawei’s Teacher Su Guanzhe also emphasizes this approach.

Conceptual Thinking involves seeing the essence through phenomena, a skill that helps in understanding the world and oneself. This ability is fundamental for personal growth and clarity.

Personal Insights

Understanding concepts is akin to understanding the world. Leslie Cheung’s lyrics, “I am what I am, a firework of a different color,” reflect the importance of individuality and value output. To live a brilliant and independent life, embracing unique characteristics is key.

By focusing on concepts and logic, you can achieve greater clarity and personal breakthroughs, making your journey through life more meaningful and insightful.
entry.summary: Explore the role of concepts and logic in personal breakthroughs and understanding the world, inspired by Einstein’s insights and modern approaches to conceptual thinking.

entry.title: Embracing Negative Entropy: The Role of Pain in Growth
entry.content:
Embracing Negative Entropy: The Role of Pain in Growth

Understanding Pain and Growth

Pain represents both physical suffering and profound emotional impact. It is a vital part of the human experience, often intertwined with growth. True personal development requires stepping outside of our comfort zones, as remaining within them for too long can lead to complacency, avoidance of reality, and a lack of crisis awareness.

Schrödinger’s Insight on Entropy

Erwin Schrödinger once said, “Living is fighting against the increase of entropy; life thrives on negative entropy.” This quote, both pessimistic and romantic, highlights the idea that life is a constant battle against disorder.

Embracing Pain as a Path to Growth

Embracing pain can be seen as a form of reverence for life itself. Life, with its inherent impermanence, is about moving towards death and understanding that growth often comes from overcoming challenges. Only by accepting this reality can we harness a powerful force for living.

Ways to Maintain Vitality

To keep both mind and body engaged, consider various activities such as:

  • Watching movies
  • Listening to music
  • Reading
  • Exercising

These activities help maintain a state of vitality and keep us connected to the rhythm of life.

Moving Towards a Brighter Future

Embrace the pain, cherish the insights gained, and always strive towards the light. By doing so, you will find that the future becomes increasingly bright and full of potential.

In essence, the ability to thrive amidst challenges and pain is a cornerstone of a meaningful and resilient life.
entry.summary: Explore the role of pain in personal growth and resilience, inspired by Schrödinger's concept of negative entropy and the idea that life thrives on overcoming challenges.

entry.title: Embracing Uncertainty: Lessons on Antifragility
entry.content:
Embracing Uncertainty: Lessons on Antifragility

The Nature of Uncertainty

People everywhere seek stability, yet the world is often unpredictable. The recent COVID-19 outbreak in Shanghai seemed sudden, with the lockdown extending beyond its expected end date. As new cases emerged daily and essential services struggled, the city felt like it was in chaos.

In such an uncertain environment, managing risk and being antifragile becomes crucial.

Understanding Antifragility

Antifragility, as described in Antifragile by Nassim Nicholas Taleb, refers to the ability to benefit from volatility and chaos. Unlike fragility, which is the condition of being harmed by uncertainty, antifragility means that you can avoid or even thrive in the face of challenges.

Mindset Matters

A key aspect of antifragility is having the right mindset. A positive and optimistic attitude is vital. As Nietzsche famously said, “That which does not kill me makes me stronger.”

During the pandemic, while many complained, those with responsibility stepped up to address and solve problems. The fundamental reason for failure in tough times often lies in a collapse of will and mindset.

Crisis Awareness and Preparedness

Crisis awareness is essential. For instance, stockpiling food is not about panic buying but about preparing for potential disruptions. If a lockdown extends beyond the expected date, having essential supplies can make a significant difference.

In any situation, preparing for the worst is a principle to live by. At work, if a project deadline is approaching, mentally prepare for delays and have backup plans.

Real-World Examples

Many companies struggled during the pandemic, but New Oriental, led by Yu Minhong, survived because of early preparation. Similarly, playing Texas Hold’em teaches valuable lessons about risk management. Occasionally taking risks is fine, but consistently going all-in leads to inevitable loss.

Thriving Amid Uncertainty

Uncertainty is the norm, and achieving complete certainty is nearly impossible. What we need to learn is how to thrive and grow despite unpredictability. This is a fundamental skill for navigating the complexities of life.

For a deeper understanding of these concepts, consider reading Antifragile and exploring how to develop resilience in the face of uncertainty.
entry.summary: Explore the concept of antifragility in the face of uncertainty, drawing insights from the COVID-19 pandemic and practical examples of resilience and preparedness.

entry.title: Suddenly Looking Back, That Person Is at the Lantern Festival
entry.content:
“State·Landscape”

Recently, discussions about “higher dimensions” often refer to an elevation in one’s state of mind.

One of my favorite Zen sayings is:

“Seeing a mountain is just seeing a mountain, seeing water is just seeing water;

Seeing a mountain is no longer just a mountain, seeing water is no longer just water;

Seeing a mountain is once again seeing a mountain, seeing water is once again seeing water.”

At various turning points in life, I like to reflect on this saying. When I first read it during my school years, I understood it, but gradually realized that I only grasped it on a superficial level. After living for half my life and experiencing many things, I found that much of the wisdom behind it comes from “experience.”

When new things appear, our first reaction is often to see only the surface—this is a mountain, this is water. Curiosity might drive us to explore, or these things might simply intrude into our lives.

When you delve deeper into the characteristics of these things, you might discover they differ from your initial perception.

A good example from life: We have learned so many Chinese characters since elementary school. While recognizing characters in paragraphs and sentences is straightforward, seeing a single character in isolation might suddenly feel unfamiliar, even leading to doubts about whether it’s written correctly.

Many aspects of life and work are similar. What seems like a simple job might become more complex when actually undertaken. Can you do it well? Can you maintain it year after year, day after day? When time is added into the equation, things might become less straightforward, and you may find yourself re-evaluating them.

When you start to seriously address a matter; when you analyze it thoroughly from beginning to end, from inside to out; and even when you take it on as a lifelong pursuit, you will become increasingly at ease.

Mr. Wang Guowei also summarized the three realms of life:

  1. Last night’s west wind withered the green tree; climbing the high tower alone, gazing at the road to the horizon.
  2. The belt grows wider, yet no regret; I have withered away for her.
  3. Searching for him a thousand times in the crowd, suddenly looking back, that person is at the lantern festival.

In Chinese discussions of states, people often say that they are only to be felt and not explained. This may be because the experience is personal, and only one’s own experience can reveal its true essence.

The Zen Master Huineng said:

“Without a Bodhi tree, and the bright mirror is not a platform;

Originally, there is nothing, where can dust cling?”

Perhaps after you have traveled through landscapes, looking back, you will find that it is all but a gentle cloud, which is the essence of completeness.
entry.summary:
“State·Landscape”

Recently, discussions about “higher dimensions” often refer to an elevation in one’s state of mind.

One o

entry.title: The Essence of Education: Uncovering Potential Within
entry.content:
The Essence of Education: Uncovering Potential Within

A Profound Metaphor

A few days ago, I came across a short video that discussed a powerful question: “What is the essence of education?” The message was both simple and profound, and I’d like to share it with you.

When Michelangelo completed the statue of David, he was astounded, and so was everyone else.

A journalist asked Michelangelo, “How did you create such a magnificent work?”

He didn’t answer immediately. Instead, he pondered for a long time before carefully responding:

“It wasn’t I who created this great work; David was already inside the stone. I simply set him free.”

These words encapsulate the timeless essence of education:

“Every person has a better self, perhaps even a great self, within them. Every child undoubtedly possesses such a self.”

The Role of the Educator: A Guide to Discovery

When it comes to education, I believe it’s essential to consider two key roles: the educator and the learner.

For educators—whether they are teachers or mentors—encouragement and discovery should be at the forefront. Just like in Michelangelo’s story, the idea that “David” was already within the stone suggests that educators are not creators but guides. They play a vital role in helping to reveal the potential already present within each individual. The future will belong to the younger generation, as this is ingrained in the very fabric of our species.

Many educators, unfortunately, prefer to impose authority and control through their status, which can stifle creativity and self-expression. Young people have their own consciousness and view issues from different perspectives. It’s crucial to communicate with them as equals, focusing on the logic and substance of the matter at hand.

Educators hold their positions because they have taken steps ahead, possessing more experience or specialized skills. However, these “experiences” or “skills” are not infallible and should always be open to discussion and re-evaluation. Through this process, educators hope that learners will ultimately acquire and refine these experiences to create new value.

The Role of the Learner: Building Confidence

For learners, the most crucial element is “confidence”—the belief that they are unique and capable of greatness. However, confidence should be neither blind nor arrogant; it must be rooted in continuous self-awareness, learning, and a commitment to a scientific and dialectical approach to life.

I believe two insights are particularly important for learners:

  1. Success is Built on Resilience and Deliberate Practice
    The more valuable something is, the more effort and resistance it usually involves. I’ve always believed that easily obtained successes are rare; occasional success might come from sheer luck, but one cannot rely on such rare events. Instead, it’s essential to persist through failures, to enhance one’s abilities across multiple domains, and to strengthen the capacity for survival and growth. This ability requires constant learning and adaptation—lifelong learning for lifelong growth.

  2. Positive Psychological Feedback is Key
    Success is not always guaranteed, but it is often possible. Positive psychological feedback, such as praise and encouragement, can sometimes make the difference in persevering through difficult times. However, it’s equally important to recognize “sunk costs.” If too much time and effort have been invested in something without success, it may be wise to reconsider further investment—this is a common lesson in entrepreneurship.

The Path Forward: Self-Discovery and Beyond

Both educators and learners must first gain a clear understanding of themselves before they can effectively understand others and the organizations they are part of. The ultimate goal of education is to discover our true selves and to help uncover the “true self” in others, ensuring that human civilization continues to progress and evolve.
entry.summary: Explore the true essence of education through the metaphor of Michelangelo's David. Understand the roles of educators and learners in the journey of self-discovery and lifelong learning.

entry.title: The Legacy of History and Our Remarkable Ancestors
entry.content:
The Legacy of History and Our Remarkable Ancestors

Why Were Our Ancestors So Remarkable?

What made those great ancestors, philosophers, and scientists so extraordinary? Civilization progresses in stages, and each era’s influential figures solved problems by standing on the shoulders of the giants who came before them. This ongoing development and transmission of knowledge is one of the core reasons why humanity must continue to evolve.

Take, for example, the philosophical and rational thinking of predecessors like Socrates and Aristotle. Their foundational work in logic laid the groundwork upon which all subsequent science is built. Without their contributions, the trajectory of human knowledge might have been vastly different.

The Timeless Questions of Every Generation

I believe that every generation of humanity grapples with a few fundamental questions:

  1. Who am I?
    This is an exploration of self-awareness, a journey inward to understand one’s own identity and purpose.

  2. Who am I with you?
    This question explores social relationships and the interconnectedness of individuals within a community or society.

  3. What is the universe?
    From the vastness of astrophysics to the intricacies of microscopic particles, this is an exploration of the world we inhabit and our place within it.

The Evolution of Thought and Technology

Ways of thinking can be cultivated and structured around first principles. Cognitive development requires accumulation over time. Throughout different eras—be it ancient times, the Stone Age, the Mechanical Age, the Electrical Age, or the Information Age—each generation has discovered or solved problems that were continually advanced based on the technological and intellectual productivity of their time.

When we consider individuals within the grand flow of human development, the emergence of great figures is inevitable. These individuals are products of their environment, shaped by the unique circumstances of their time. The reason why they were great and we may not yet be lies in the technological advantages and cognitive awakening brought about by their era’s specific challenges and opportunities.

Reflecting on Einstein’s Perspective

I recently revisited Einstein’s “The World As I See It” and have been reflecting on what great minds like him were doing during their time, and why they thought the way they did. Einstein’s assertion that “the world is comprehensible” resonates deeply with me.

Today, many unresolved questions remain, and scientists continue to research and explore the fundamental nature of our world. The legacy of our ancestors drives this ongoing quest for knowledge and understanding.
entry.summary: Explore the legacy of great ancestors, philosophers, and scientists who shaped civilization by standing on the shoulders of giants. Discover the timeless questions that each generation seeks to answer.

entry.title: Dimensions | Point·Line·Plane | Motion
entry.content:
To an ant, the world might seem flat. Humans also once believed the Earth was flat.

A line is formed by connecting a series of points, and a plane is made up of multiple lines.

When we talk about “dimension elevation” or “dimension reduction,” what exactly is this dimension?

Why does the view from a mountaintop make all other mountains seem small? Why do humans easily mock the pitiful nature of insects? And why do we sometimes look back at our past selves and find our previous ignorance laughable?

We often place great importance on a single exam, the success or failure of a startup, or the joys and sorrows of life. Are these things important? Yes, they are, because they form the lines of your life experience, shaping the three-dimensional you.

“Material is in motion.”

This is a fundamental law. We often say that we should learn to view problems dynamically. It’s easy to see that when you move a point from A to B, it forms a line. When you move a line from A to B, it forms a plane. When that plane moves from A to B, it becomes a solid.

“Material motion requires force, and moving against resistance consumes energy.”

One day, a person who felt aimless suddenly realized they needed a dream. This “realization,” whether driven by external or internal factors, is a kind of “force,” propelling them toward that dream.

What is a dream? A dream is a leap in one’s life, a determination and journey from a lower dimension to a higher one.

What is resistance? It is the countless energy expenditures required to reach a higher dimension, representing the various experiences of life.

Life should be three-dimensional. It requires curiosity, continuous exploration, and constant experiences.

Life is a journey of cultivation, a process of pursuing the other shore.

When you understand that many times you need to observe problems dynamically, from a comprehensive, three-dimensional, and even higher-dimensional perspective, you may find that the answers to many things become clear.
entry.summary:
To an ant, the world might seem flat. Humans also once believed the Earth was flat.

A line is formed by connecting a series of poi
